cited_text: If you're stacking a lot of things on top of each other, each one of those things becomes less important. So if you have 10 things, each one of them is one tenth as important as one by itself. So if you're making something and you want the least amount involved, those things have to be really critically curated because they're doing the work of everything and nothing is hidden.
response_text: The arithmetic of attention. Ten elements means each carries one-tenth the weight.

cited_text: The fact that I didn't know what I was doing allowed it to be true to what hip hop was. Whereas all of the records that were coming out, the few and far between 12 inch singles, every few weeks, they were made by professionals who made other kinds of music. They weren't made by hip hop people. So an outsider with experience was making what they thought hip hop was. But if you went to the club, you knew what it really was.
response_text: The professionals making hip-hop records weren't hip-hop people — they made a Hollywood version of it. Rubin's ignorance was the competitive advantage.

cited_text: And I thought the word meant to build up. Like, I think of production as building. And really what I was doing was taking apart and reducing. I thought maybe reducing would be reduced by. Is more accurate in this case.
response_text: At 18 or 19, he renamed his credit from "produced by" to "reduced by" because the word was more accurate.

cited_text: **Speaker B**: Sometimes, for the sake of the whole work, removing things about it that you really love is part of the process.
response_text: His ruthless edit framework: if you need to cut 30%, don't whittle down to 70% — cut to 40%, then build back up to 70%. The over-cut forces a cleaner understanding of what actually matters.

cited_text: If you have 100% and you know at the end you want to have 70% of what you have, like you have 30% too much, instead of whittling down that 30 to get to the 70, I would say reduce it to 40%. Let's say force yourself to get to 40% and then add back what's. What's needed to get to the 70. And it works in a different way.
response_text: His ruthless edit framework: if you need to cut 30%, don't whittle down to 70% — cut to 40%, then build back up to 70%. The over-cut forces a cleaner understanding of what actually matters.

cited_text: **Speaker B**: It feels like his entire life is centered around writing words. He's totally preoccupied with that. So he always has a notebook. He's always making little notes. response_text: Describing Eminem: always has notebooks, always making notes in tiny letters. Asked if he's working on new songs, Eminem said no — just keeping active in the skill set. 90% of what he writes will never become a song.

cited_text: I have to force myself to do it. But I do force myself. My demeanor would be to do nothing, Rick.

cited_text: That's the feeling, that moment of discovery where it goes from nothing to something really good. cited_text: Like if you only wait for inspiration, it won't ever come. Like you have to work and be there and show up. If you're not. If you're not in the practice of allowing the thing to happen, it won't happen. Doesn't mean it will. Just because you do show up doesn't mean it will happen. But if you don't show up, it won't happen.
response_text: Being in the practice is a necessary but not sufficient condition. Showing up doesn't guarantee the thing happens — but not showing up guarantees it won't.

cited_text: **Speaker B**: He said something really interesting that I thought really summed it up, which was, jimmy is in the banking business. These are his words. He said, I'm in the banking business and you're in the church business. And that's the difference.
response_text: Jimmy Iovine's own words, describing the difference between himself and Rubin: one runs on passion and belief, the other on bottom line and what's good for business.

cited_text: I think most of what I do is not really about music. I happen to work in music, but it's not about the music.
response_text: His studio, Shangri-La, has the same aesthetic as his records, the objects he buys — it all fits into a worldview, not a genre.

cited_text: **Speaker B**: You're at Shangri La and you walk through the space and you see it doesn't feel like other places you've been. cited_text: Each, each of those installments are real. So it's always true. It doesn't mean that that's who you are forever. It's who you are in that moment. It's really freeing. It's helpful for an artist to think that way because usually, especially when we're younger, we think the thing that we make is, this is my magnum opus, and this is going to define me for the rest of my life, and it's a daunting hill to climb. But when you realize it's like, this is just the one today, and we're going to make another one tomorrow, and hopefully the one tomorrow is going to be as good or better than the one today, what's true today?
response_text: Treating work as diary entries: no regrets about the past, because that was real at the time. Especially useful for younger artists who treat each thing as a potential magnum opus — a daunting hill that paralyses them.
